coredata.dc:title: Spontaneous assembly of bilayer perovskite crystals for built-in p-n homojunction
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Ruihao
Xu, Xiangjun
Jin, Bowen
Liu, Siwen
Cao, Jinguo
Wang, Shimin
Wang, Erjing
Chu, Liang
Wu, Congcong
coredata.dc:description: Developing sufficient dynamics for charge separation and transfer is critical to improve photovoltaic performance. Normally, dynamics can be modulated by band bending via surface engineering, but this has typically been negligible in the p-n homojunction for boosted perovskite solar cells. Herein, we adopt carbon dots as an addition to induce a bilayer p-n perovskite homojunction to improve interfacial charge transfer within perovskite solar cells. Notably, the inclusion of carbon dots induces the spontaneous assembly of a bilayer structure, where the upper layer is enriched with dots and the underlying layer remains pristine perovskite. This unique arrangement creates a p-n homojunction, accompanied by an internal electric field, which allows for modulating energy levels and facilitating charge-carrier separation and transfer. Benefiting from the p-n homojunction, the power conversion efficiency of the perovskite solar cells increases from 18.57% to 21.09%. Additionally, the hydrophobic nature of the carbon dots considerably enhances the humidity stability of the solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: p-n homojunction
carbon dots
perovskite bilayer
charge recombination
energy level

E.W. acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 52073082 ). L.C. acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 52172205 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The formation of bilayer perovskite film
(A) Mechanism diagram of bilayer perovskite formation through the evaporation-driven flow of ACN.
(B and C) Cross-section SEM images of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films. Scale bar: 200 nm.
(D and E) Surface SEM images of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films, labeled serial (i). Scale bar: 200 nm.
(F and G) EDS mapping images of O element of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films, labeled serial (ii).
(H and I) ToF-SIMS depth profiling and three-dimensional profile signal of C element of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films.
(J) ToF-SIMS depth profiling of S element of the CD-incorporated perovskite films.
Energy band modulation by CD incorporation
(A) Schematics of the interaction between CDs and perovskite crystals.
(B) UPS spectra of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films.
(C) Energy band diagram of the CD-incorporated perovskites.
(D and E) KPFM images of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films. Scale bar: 1 μm.
(F) KPFM profile of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films.
Charge-carrier transfer kinetics of the CD-incorporated perovskite film
(A and B) Steady-state and time-resolved PL spectra of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films.
(C) SCLC plots of the electron-only devices based on the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films.
(D and E) Dark-current and EIS plots of the devices based on the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films.
(F) Illustration of built-in p-n junction in the CD-incorporated perovskite film.
Photovoltaic performance and long-term stability of PSCs
(A and B) J-V characteristics and IPCE spectra of the PSCs based on the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films.
(C) Steady-state photocurrent density and power output at the maximum power point.
(D and E) XRD patterns of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films that are continuously illuminated under 1 sun condition.
(F and G) XRD patterns of the control and CD-incorporated perovskite films that are aging in ambient air (RH: 60% ± 5%) for 76 days.
(H) Evolution of the perovskite film surface over time when the droplet contacts the perovskite surface.
(I) Long-term stability of the unencapsulated control and target PSCs aging in ambient environment for 1,400 h. Error bars, standard deviation (SD) for three devices' measurements.

head.ce:title: Spontaneous assembly of bilayer perovskite crystals for built-in p-n homojunction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Carbon Neutrality and New Energy, School of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Carbon Neutrality and New Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Carbon Neutrality and New Energy, School of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing sufficient dynamics for charge separation and transfer is critical to improve photovoltaic performance. Normally, dynamics can be modulated by band bending via surface engineering, but this has typically been negligible in the p-n homojunction for boosted perovskite solar cells. Herein, we adopt carbon dots as an addition to induce a bilayer p-n perovskite homojunction to improve interfacial charge transfer within perovskite solar cells. Notably, the inclusion of carbon dots induces the spontaneous assembly of a bilayer structure, where the upper layer is enriched with dots and the underlying layer remains pristine perovskite. This unique arrangement creates a p-n homojunction, accompanied by an internal electric field, which allows for modulating energy levels and facilitating charge-carrier separation and transfer. Benefiting from the p-n homojunction, the power conversion efficiency of the perovskite solar cells increases from 18.57% to 21.09%. Additionally, the hydrophobic nature of the carbon dots considerably enhances the humidity stability of the solar cells.

Incorporating carbon dots into a perovskite p-n homojunction causes a bilayer structure to form spontaneously, with the top layer containing an abundance of carbon dots and the bottom layer maintaining its original perovskite composition. Ma et al. show that the configuration creates an internal electric field, facilitating the adjustment of energy levels and promoting efficient separation and transfer of charge carriers.

ce:section.ce:para:
During the past decade, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have gained significant attention as a promising photovoltaic technology due to their exceptional power conversion efficiency (PCE) and facile fabrication process. 1
,
2
,
3 Despite impressive progress, developing sufficient dynamics for charge separation and transfer remains crucial in order to further enhance the PCE of PSCs 4
,
5 and the deployment of modules. 6
,
7 The charge recombination mainly occurs at the perovskite film interfaces, either between the perovskite and the electron transport layer (PVK/ETL) or the perovskite and the hole transport layer (PVK/HTL), due to the presence of interface defects and energy-level mismatches. 8
,
9
,
10
,
11 To address this issue, constructing perovskite junctions has emerged as a promising approach. 12
,
13
,
14 These junctions facilitate energy-level matching and generate a built-in electric field at the interface, thereby promoting carrier separation and transfer.

Constructing perovskite junctions presents significant challenges, primarily due to the difficulties in synthesizing two adjacent layers of perovskites without compromising the integrity of the underlying layer. As a result, various solvent-free synthetic methods have been utilized to fabricate perovskite homojunctions. Dänekamp et al. utilized the vapor deposition technique to precisely control the sublimation rate of precursors. This was achieved by ensuring an abundance of PbI2 or methylammonium iodide (MAI) in the perovskite layer, endowing it with an n- or p-type characteristic for the formation of the homojunction. 15 Cui et al. utilized a spin-coating technique for depositing the underlying n-type perovskite layer and subsequently employed vacuum vapor deposition to fabricate the overlying p-type perovskite layer. This sequential approach allowed for the creation of a perovskite homojunction. 16 Liu et al. incorporated the antioxidant vitamin D2 (VD2) into the perovskite layer, effectively altering the surface properties from n-type to p-type. This modification resulted in the successful formation of perovskite homojunction structures. 17 However, these reported strategies for constructing homojunctions are either time consuming or complicated and can lead to crystalline defects due to uncontrollable ion impregnation. Consequently, a more feasible approach that involves simple preparation techniques and passivating the intracrystalline trap states is needed to construct perovskite homojunctions.

Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with functional groups were synthesized and incorporated into the perovskite precursor solution to induce a distinct bilayer structure with the CD-rich perovskite upper layer (p-type) and the lower layer consisting of perovskite (n-type). This spontaneous assembly leads to the formation of a homojunction with adjustable thickness, thereby enhancing carrier separation and transport at the interface. As a result, the modified device achieved a PCE of up to 21.09%, compared to 18.50% for the unmodified device. Furthermore, the hydrophobic properties of the CDs and their even distribution throughout the upper perovskite film enhance the overall environmental stability of the device. After 1,400 h of exposure to ambient air, the device maintained 90% of its initial PCE, further underlining its improved stability.

CDs were synthesized utilizing 2,2′-dithiodibenzoic acid (DTSA) and the diamino substituent tetraphenylethylene (TPE-2NH2) as starting materials, as shown in Figure S1. In order to determine the functional groups on the surface of CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) transmission spectra were measured, as depicted in Figure S2. The absorption peak observed at 1,170 cm−1 signifies the stretching vibration of C−O, indicating the presence of oxygen (O)-containing functional groups, specifically carboxylic acids, in the CDs. Moreover, the bending vibration of C−S at 738 cm−1 suggests the existence of sulfur (S) in the CDs, while the absorption peak at 1,280 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of C−N, indicating the successful incorporation of nitrogen (N) in the CDs. 18 Additionally, the observed stretching vibration peaks around 3,200 cm−1 indicate the presence of amino groups on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, the electronic states of the S, C, N, and O elements in the CDs were analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements, as depicted in Figure S3. The S 1s spectrum of the CDs displayed two distinct peaks at 165.3 and 165.7 eV, representing S–C and S–S bonds, respectively. 19 The C 1s spectrum exhibited peaks at 284.7 and 285.5 eV, indicating the presence of C=C and C−N bonds, respectively. 20 Analysis of the N 1s spectrum revealed the existence of pyridine N (398.9 eV), pyrrole N (399.7 eV), and amino N (400.6 eV), indicating the presence of amino groups on the surface of the CDs. Moreover, the O 1s spectrum exhibited peaks at 532.4 and 532.9 eV, corresponding to C–O–C and C–OH bonds, respectively. 21

For the production of perovskite thin films, a solvent system containing ethanol (with 33 wt % methylamine [MA]) and acetonitrile (ACN) was utilized. 22
,
23 With this system, MA is capable of entering into the perovskite lattice and forming liquefied perovskite intermediate cluster (PIC). 24
,
25 When the CDs were added to the solution and mixed thoroughly, they could be uniformly distributed with the PIC in the mixed solution. However, these CDs apparently possess relatively smaller mass relative to the PIC with heavy elements of the same size, which allows the rapidly evaporating solvent (ACN and EtOH exhibit volatility) in the subsequent process to “drive” the relatively lighter CDs toward the top of the wet film (consistent with the direction of evaporation). During solvent evaporation, the PIC gradually forms perovskite grains. Notably, the bottom PIC can form large pure MAPbI3 perovskite grains, whereas the top PIC can only form relatively smaller CDs-MAPbI3 grains due to the restricted crystal growth caused by the interaction of CDs with MAPbI3 (as demonstrated later via Figures S9 and S10). The corresponding film formation processes are shown schematically in Figure 1 A. As a result, the CDs primarily accumulate in the upper layer of the overall film and form a bilayer perovskite structure, with the upper layer containing a high concentration of CDs and the lower layer devoid of CDs.

To be noted, films and/or devices incorporated without and with CDs are respectively denoted as “control” and “target” in almost all figures (including those in the supplemental information). Figures 1B and 1C depict the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the perovskite films without and with the incorporation of CDs, respectively. The presence of CDs serves as a heterogeneous nucleation center, expediting the nucleation rate of perovskite crystals during the crystallization process. 26
,
27 Consequently, the wet film containing CDs exhibits a formation of smaller and more compact grains in the upper layer of perovskite. Conversely, the lower layer of perovskite, which has fewer CDs, displays larger grains akin to the pristine perovskite composition. The surface morphology of the perovskite films can be observed in Figures 1D and 1E. Consistent with the findings, the perovskite film incorporating CDs exhibited smaller and denser grains on its surface compared to the pristine perovskite film (control sample). The surface and cross-sectional SEM images of perovskite films with different concentrations of CDs are shown in Figure S4. The introduction of CDs leads to the formation of a bilayer structure in the perovskite, and when the concentration of CDs reaches 1.5 mg/mL, the grains accumulate in a disordered manner. Therefore, 1 mg/mL is considered the optimal concentration of CDs in the following analysis. The bilayer-structured perovskite possesses smaller upper perovskite grains that effectively occupy the grain boundaries between larger perovskite grains. This compaction allows for a closer packing of the perovskite grains, which is expected to favor the interfacial charge transfer. Figure S5 displays the atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the pristine perovskite films and CD-incorporated perovskite films. The CD-incorporated film, with the addition of CDs, exhibited a root-mean-square (RMS) roughness of 2.58 nm, indicating a more uniform and denser distribution of perovskite grains compared to the pristine perovskite film (3.72 nm).

Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was employed to analyze the elemental distribution of oxygen (O), iodine (I), and lead (Pb) on the surface of the perovskite film. Figures 1F and 1G depict the EDS mapping results for the O element (green) on the surface of the two perovskite films. It was evident that the original perovskite film displayed a negligible O element, while the film containing CDs clearly exhibited a notable signal of O, which is ascribed to the O-containing functional groups of the CDs, indicating the presence of CDs on the surface of perovskite film. Figure S6 presents the EDS mappings of I (yellow) and Pb (red) on the perovskite film surfaces, which are similar for both the perovskite films with and without CDs. In order to examine the distribution of elements in the bilayer structure, we utilized time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) analysis to uncover the chemical differences between the upper and lower layers. The ToF-SIMS depth profile depicted in Figure 1H demonstrates that during the initial 180 s of sputtering, the perovskite film integrated with CDs exhibits a higher level of carbon (C) signal compared to the pristine perovskite film. Subsequently, both films display a similar profile of carbon distribution, confirming that the CDs are predominantly localized in the upper layer of the perovskite film. Figure 1I illustrates the three-dimensional profile signals of the C element in the pristine perovskite film and the CD-incorporated perovskite films, clearly showing the uniform distribution of C concentration in the pristine film, while there is a higher concentration of C in the upper layer of the CD-incorporated film. As CDs were synthesized using a compound rich in sulfur (DTSA), the signal of the sulfur (S) element can serve as an indicator to trace the presence of CDs across the perovskite film. As presented in Figure 1J, during the initial period of sputtering, the CD-incorporated perovskite film exhibits a strong S signal, which diminishes significantly as sputtering time increases. This further supports the finding that CDs are situated in the upper layer of the perovskite film.

The crystal morphology of the CD-incorporated perovskites was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Figure S7. The TEM image revealed a nano-core-shell structure, where CDs uniformly coated the outer surface of the perovskite crystals. In order to investigate the effect of CDs on the crystal structure and absorption properties, the XRD patterns were compared between films without and with CDs, as depicted in Figure S8. The diffraction peaks observed at 14.1° and 28.2°, which correspond to the (110) and (220) crystal planes, respectively, were found to be similar in both the CD-incorporated perovskite film and the pristine film. This suggests that the inclusion of CDs has a minimal effect on the crystal structure. Additionally, the UV-visible (UV-vis) absorbance of the CD-incorporated perovskite films exhibited no significant change in comparison to the pristine films. This indicates that the addition of CDs did not alter the optical band gap of the films (Figure S8).

To effectively analyze the interface, it is essential to emphasize the chemical interaction resulting from the incorporation of CDs within the upper layer of the perovskite layer. Figure S9 displays the FT-IR analysis for CDs, pristine perovskite (MAPbI3), and CD-incorporated perovskite (CDs-MAPbI3). Notably, in the CDs-MAPbI3 sample, absorption peaks were observed at 1,170 and 738 cm−1, which originated from the C−O and C−S vibrations of CDs. Additionally, a shift from 1,280 to 1,260 cm−1 in the bending vibration of C−N of CDs was observed when combined with perovskite, indicating that the C−N bond participates in the chemical interaction between CDs and MAPbI3. To further analyze the chemical bonding, XPS was compared between the perovskite films with and without CDs. In the pristine MAPbI3 film, the Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2 energy levels exhibited two prominent peaks at 138.4 and 143.4 eV, respectively. However, upon introducing CDs, the binding energies of Pb 4f shifted to 138.6 and 143.5 eV, respectively. Similarly, the binding energies of I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2, corresponding to energy levels of 619.2 and 630.7 eV, were shifted to 619.5 and 631.0 eV, respectively, after the addition of CDs (Figure S10). These shifts in binding energies for Pb 4f and I 3d indicate that both Pb and I ions are engaged in chemical interactions with CDs. 28
,
29
,
30
Figure 2 A illustrates that CDs form a nano-core-shell structure with perovskite crystals and that the rich functional groups of CDs may coordinate with uncoordinated iodine and lead ions on the surface of perovskite. These interactions can be used to modify the perovskite interface, thereby passivating the trap state and eliminating carrier recombination.

The introduction of CDs into the perovskite film is expected to affect the energy band at its interface. To investigate this further, UV photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was measured to analyze the energy bands of the perovskite film and determine the interfacial energy band alignment. In Figure 2B, we can see that the ionization potential (IP) of the original perovskite film is 5.56 eV, while the work function (WF) is 4.95 eV. After adding CDs, the IP value of the perovskite film remained basically unchanged, but the WF increased to 5.15 eV. The positions of the Fermi levels (EF) of perovskite films with and without CDs can be determined based on the values of the WF, with reference to the vacuum level. It can be observed that the EF of the pristine film is higher than its valence band maximum (VBM) by 0.61 eV, while the EF of the CD-incorporated perovskite film is higher than its VBM by 0.43 eV. This indicates that the latter is closer to the VBM and that the film exhibits p-type characteristics. Therefore, the overall perovskite film with the addition of CDs consists of the top CD-incorporated perovskite (p-type) and the bottom pristine perovskite (n-type) (Figure 2C). 17
,
31 To validate the alteration in surface potential, Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) was utilized to measure the perovskite film without and with CDs, as depicted in Figures 2D and 2E. The surface potential can be calculated according to Equation 1.
(Equation 1)
V
P r o b e
=
W
P r o b e
–
W
S a m p l e
e

Consistent with the UPS results, the average surface potential of the pristine perovskite film was measured to be 486 mV, while the perovskite film with CDs showed a surface potential of 305 mV. Moreover, we performed linear scanning on both types of perovskite films, as seen in Figure 2F. The surface potential of the CD-incorporated perovskite film consistently presented a decrease of 200 mV compared to the original perovskite film. This further confirms that the EF of the CD-incorporated perovskite film is closer to the VBM. As a result, the pristine surface tends to exhibit an n-type behavior due to its EF being relatively closer to the conduction band minimum (CBM), whereas the surface with the inclusion of CDs demonstrates p-type characteristics. Consequently, the bilayer structure of the CD-incorporated perovskite film results in the formation of an intrinsic p-n homojunction, where the upper layer containing CDs exhibits p-type behavior while the lower layer consisting of pristine perovskite demonstrates n-type behavior.

The formation of a p-n homojunction at the interface afforded a built-in electrical field and is expected to promote interfacial carrier transfer. To demonstrate the hole transfer capability for the CD-incorporated perovskite interface, we conducted photoluminescence (PL) measurements for the device with an architecture of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass/PVK/HTL, as shown in Figure 3 A. The emission intensity of the perovskite film with CDs is much lower than that of the pristine film due to the quenching of the PL, suggesting that the holes are more easily transferred from the CD-incorporated perovskite to the HTL layer. Furthermore, the time-resolved PL (TRPL) was measured to calculate the charge-carrier lifetime, as shown in Figure 3B. By fitting the decay curve using a double exponential model, the average lifetime defined as τave can be calculated according to Equation 2:
(Equation 2)
f
( t )
=
A 1
exp
(
− t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
− t
τ 2
)
+ B ,
where τ
1 is the fast-decay lifetime component and τ
2 is the slow-decay lifetime component. A
1 and A
2 are the corresponding decay amplitudes, and B is a constant. 32
,
33 The CD-containing perovskite film exhibited a carrier lifetime of 9.43 ns, while the pristine perovskite film had a lifetime of 13.55 ns. The shorter lifetime in the CD-containing film further verifies the faster carrier transfer and more efficient carrier collection by the HTL compared to the pristine perovskite film.

Furthermore, we conducted measurements of space-charge-limited current (SCLC) to analyze the kinetics of charge carriers. Initially, the SCLC in the hole-only device was measured with an architecture of FTO/PTAA/PVK/Spiro-OMeTAD/Au, as shown in Figure 3C. In the low-bias range, there is a linear correlation between current density and applied bias due to an ohmic response in this region. Beyond the trap-filling limit voltage (V
TFL ), the current density exhibited a non-linear increase as the bias increased, suggesting that the injected charge occupied the trap. 34
,
35 Based on this behavior, the trap density (Nt) of the perovskite film can be calculated according to Equation 3:
(Equation 3)
V
T F L
=
e
N t
L 2
2 ε
ε 0
where e refers to the electron charge, L represents the thickness of the perovskite film, ε
0 denotes the vacuum dielectric constant, and ε corresponds to the relative dielectric constant of MAPbI3 (with ε = 32). The CDs-MAPbI3 presents a lower trapping density of 7.5 × 1015 cm−3 compared to pristine MAPbI3 (Nt = 1.0 × 1016 cm−3). This difference can be attributed to the presence of C−O, C−S, and C–N bonds on the surface of CDs, which facilitate electronic interactions with cation vacancies. Additionally, the amino groups in CDs can interact with I to form N−H−I, both of which contribute to the effective passivation of perovskite crystals and consequently reduce trap density. To validate the passivation effect of CDs, steady-state PL and TRPL measurements were conducted on perovskite films deposited on bare glass (Figure S11). The luminescence intensity was found to increase with increasing concentration of CDs, peaking at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. In addition, the carrier lifetime of the perovskite film with CDs (77.75 ns) was obviously longer than that of the pristine perovskite film (31.02 ns). In addition, the current density-voltage (J-V) curves of intact PSC devices in the dark were measured, as shown in Figure 3D, indicating that the CDs-MAPbI3 exhibits a smaller leakage current than the control device. These results suggest that the presence of CDs effectively reduces trap density and minimizes non-radiative processes.

Furthermore, the charge-transport properties in the perovskite films were estimated in the trap-free region (n = 2) of SCLC plots, and the carrier mobility, μ, can be derived from Equation 4:
(Equation 4)
J =
9 8
μ
ε ε
0
V 2
L 3

The mobility of CDs-MAPbI3 film (2.5 × 10−3 cm2 V−1 s−1) was found to be 10 times higher than that of the pristine MAPb3 film (2.2 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1), owing to the presence of the built-in electric field, which enhances the carrier separation and thus mobility. Additionally, we constructed electron-only devices with FTO/TiO2/PVK/phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM)/Ag structure to investigate the electron transport properties, as shown in Figure S12. Consistent with the hole-only device, the pristine MAPbI3 exhibited an Nt of 9.6 × 1015 cm−3, whereas the CDs-MAPbI3 film displayed a comparatively lower Nt of 6.52 × 1015 cm−3. Concurrently, the electron transport mobility of CDs-MAPbI3 (2.0 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1) is nearly 10 times higher than that of the pristine MAPbI3 (1.9 × 10−5 cm2 V−1 s−1). To assess the impact of CDs on the transfer of charges across interfaces, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to examine the kinetics of interfacial charge transfer. Figure 3E displays the Nyquist plots of the two devices without and with CD incorporation. These measurements were taken under 1 sun illumination at a bias voltage of 1 V. Notably, the device incorporating CDs exhibited a smaller charge-transfer resistance (59 Ω) compared to the pristine perovskite device (124 Ω). This improvement in charge-transfer efficiency can be attributed to the enhanced charge mobility facilitated by the p-n homojunction formed by the incorporation of CDs.

To understand this enhanced charge mobility mechanism, we exhibit the band-edge arrangement of n-type and p-type perovskites, and the carrier drift and diffusion within them, in Figure 3F. One can see that the n-type perovskite possesses a lower CBM and VBM than the p-type perovskite, which implies that electrons drift from the latter to the former, while holes drift in the opposite direction. Unfortunately, there are also inevitable carrier diffusions within the bilayer perovskites, whose directions are opposite to the drift direction, detrimental to the photovoltaic performance of device. In our bilayer perovskites, the carrier drifts lead to a built-in electric field with direction from the n-type to p-type perovskite (consistent with the direction of hole drift), which effectively limits the diffusion of carriers. Moreover, the built-in electric field can promote carrier drifts within the bilayer perovskites that are favorable for their further transfer to the charge-transport layer. For the control film, there is no built-in electric field and thus non-limited carrier diffusion. The unnecessary carrier diffusion within controlled film will contribute to the mediocre efficiency of the device.

Figure 4 A presents the J-V characteristics of the PSCs. The initial PSC demonstrates to an open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 1.02 V, a short-circuit current density (JSC) of 23.01 mA cm−2, and a fill factor (FF) of 78.4%, corresponding to a PCE of 18.57%. Upon incorporation of CDs, the PCE of the PSC improves to 21.09%. The VOC increases to 1.12 V, the JSC increases marginally to 23.53 mA cm−2, and the FF improves to 80.3%. The primary factors contributing to the enhanced PCE are the improved VOC and FF. The incorporation of CDs passivates trap states, reducing non-radiative recombination and consequently improving the VOC. Additionally, the mobility of charges improves with CDs, resulting in a decrease in the series resistance of the PSC, which boosts the FF. Figure S13 shows the J-V characteristics of PSCs with varying concentrations of CDs. Notably, the introduction of CDs enhances the photovoltaic performance, with PCE increasing as the CD concentration increases. The maximum improvement is achieved at a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL (Table S1). To systematically evaluate the impact of the incorporated CDs on the device performance, statistical performance tests were conducted. As shown in Figure S14, the VOC, JSC, and FF of the target devices, as well as the corresponding PCEs, are obviously higher than those of the control devices. To confirm the accuracy of the current density derived from the J-V curve, we conducted incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements, as shown in Figure 4B. The integrated current density obtained from the IPCE measurements matches the values obtained from the J-V characteristics, with the pristine PSCs exhibiting a current density of 22.43 mA cm−2 and the CD-incorporated PSCs showcasing 23.02 mA cm−2. Furthermore, we measured the steady-state efficiency of the CD-incorporated PSCs under a potential of 0.94 V, which demonstrated a stabilized PCE of 20.7%, as depicted in Figure 4C.

The stability of the bilayer structure perovskite film was evaluated to determine its resistance against light and moisture. XRD patterns of the pristine perovskite film and the CD-incorporated perovskite film under 1 sun illumination are shown in Figures 4D and 4E. Within 1 h of illumination, the pristine film started to decompose, whereas the modified film showed no signs of decomposition even after 3 h. Five hours later, the pristine film completely decomposed, while the CD-incorporated film only exhibited slight decomposition. Furthermore, the humidity stability of both devices was evaluated by aging the perovskite film under ambient air with a relative humidity (RH) of 60% ± 5% for 76 days. The decomposition of the pristine film began after 35 days, and it was fully decomposed after 76 days. However, the CD-incorporated film showed negligible decomposition with the formation of a small amount of PbI2, indicating excellent humidity stability with the addition of CDs. The incorporation of the hydrophobic and water-resistant CDs in the top layer of the perovskite film contributes to the environmental stability improvement by preventing water ingress. To further verify the water resistance of the CD-incorporated perovskite film, a water corrosion experiment was performed as depicted in Figure 4H. Water droplets were placed on the surface of the perovskite films, and the appearance changes of the perovskite surface were examined. After 3 s, the water droplet region on the original perovskite film decomposed completely, resulting in a yellow surface color due to the formation of PbI2. 18 In contrast, the water droplet region of the CD-incorporated film showed no obvious changes, highlighting its high water-resistant feature. In addition, we conducted a stability assessment of the PSCs by subjecting the devices to ambient air (30% humidity) for a period of 1,400 h. The results, depicted in Figure 4I, indicate that the PCE of the pristine perovskite maintained 60% of its initial value after the aging process. In contrast, the PCE of the perovskite modified with CDs maintained 90% of its original value. This clearly demonstrates the significantly enhanced stability of the CD-incorporated PSCs.

In summary, we developed a spontaneous assembly route for built-in p-n perovskite homojunctions. Ascribed to the evaporation-driven flow of volatile solvent, a bilayer-structured perovskite film can be spontaneously formed, consisting of a CD-rich upper layer (p-type) and a lower n-type perovskite layer. The built-in p-n homojunction, on one hand, facilitates the interfacial charge transfer by aligning the energy levels, and on the other hand, the functional groups on CDs can effectively passivate the interfacial traps, thereby significantly enhancing the charge-carrier transfer and minimizing the charge recombination. This self-assembled homojunction results in an increase in PCE from 18.57% to 21.09%. Additionally, it enhances the environmental stability of the PSCs, with 90% of its original value retained after exposure to humid air for 1,400 h.
Further information and requests for resources should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Congcong Wu (ccwu@hubu.edu.cn).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.

The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request. All data analyzed during this study are present in the article or the supplemental information.

FTO glass, MAI, lead (II) iodide (PbI2; 99.99%), and spiro-OMeTAD (99.86%) were purchased from Advanced Election Technology. TiO2 paste (18NR-T) was obtained from Xi’an Polymer Light Technology. ACN (99.5%), ethanol (EtOH; 99.5%), and chlorobenzene (CB; 99.9%) were purchased from Aladdin. MA solution (33 wt % in absolute ethanol) was purchased from Alfa Aesar.

Precursor TPE-2NH2 was synthesized according to literature, 36 and the CDs were prepared through a hydrothermal process. First, an ethanol solution (60 mL) of dithiosalicylic acid (300 mg) and TPE-2NH2 (250 mg) was sonicated for 30 min and then was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and allowed to react at 180°C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solvent was removed through rotary evaporation. The residue was applied to a chromatographic column for purification using petroleum ether/dichloromethane as eluent for gradient elution. Finally, a red solid was obtained as the final product CDs.

The FTO glasses were cleaned with deionized water, acetone, and alcohol under ultrasonication for each 20 min, followed by drying in an oven. After that, the dried substrate was treated using UV ozone for 30 min. A compact layer of TiO2 was then spin coated on these precleaned FTO substrates at 3,000 rpm for 30 s, followed by thermal annealing at 150°C for 10 min. A mesoporous TiO2 layer was then spin coated on top of the compact layer at 6,000 rpm for 50 s. After that, the TiO2 layers were annealed at 500°C for 1 h. For the preparation of perovskite precursor solution, 0.461 g PbI2 and 0.159 g MAI were dissolved in 500 μL MA solution and dispersed under ultrasonication for 20 min. The CDs were first dissolved in ACN solution and then added to the perovskite solution at the designed concentration ratio. The MAPbI3 precursor solution was deposited on TiO2 layers at 4,000 rpm for 30 s. Spiro-OMeTAD was then spin coated at 4,000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, an 80 nm gold electrode was thermally deposited to finalize the device.

Absorption spectra were measured with a UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu). The film morphologies were examined with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (JSM-7100F, JEOL). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8A25). FT-IR spectroscopy was measured using an FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific). The steady-state PL and TRPL were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoTime 300, PicoQuant). The incident photon-to-electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) of PSCs was measured using a quantum efficiency system (300 W xenon lamp, Newport). The J-V curves of the solar cells were measured using a Keithley digital source meter (model 2400) under a solar simulator (91192-1000, Oriel) to provide AM 1.5G, 100 mW cm−2 illumination. The EIS measurements were measured under AM 1.5G illumination by an electrochemical workstation (IM6, Zennium) over a frequency range of 100 mHz–2 MHz.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: C.W. acknowledges the Key Research and Development Program of Hubei Province, China (grant no. 2022BAA096) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 62004064). E.W. acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 52073082). L.C. acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 52172205).

ce:section.ce:para: R.M. conducted experiments and theoretical analysis, data organization, investigation, and writing; X.X. synthesized CDs and provided data on CDs; and B.J. and J.C. discussed experimental and theoretical methods, writing, and overview, while S.L. provided characterization and testing support. S.W. conducted the investigation process. E.W. and L.C. analyzed the results. C.W. supervised the overall project.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental information can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xcrp.2024.101796.
